title: Modal Editing
subtitle: Why every key on the keyboard is a command
summary: Vim is modal: the same keys mean different things in different modes. *Normal mode* is your resting state.

blocks.text: You spend more time reading, navigating, and restructuring code than typing new code from scratch. Think about your last hour of editing. How much of it was actually inserting new characters? Maybe twenty percent. The rest was scrolling, searching, selecting, deleting, copying, moving, and rearranging.

Most editors give you a full keyboard for the typing — and then make you reach for the mouse, hold modifier keys, or chase menus for everything else. Vim flips this. It gives you a full keyboard for the *eighty* percent.

That is what "modal" means. In **normal mode**, every key on your keyboard is a command. {key:j} moves down. {key:w} jumps forward by a word. {key:d}{key:w} deletes a word. {key:c}{key:i}{key:"} changes the text inside quotes. No mouse. No Ctrl-Shift-Arrow. Just keystrokes.
Don't try to memorize the key sequences sprinkled through these early chapters — they're just examples, here to give the flavor of the language. Each one gets its own section later in the book.
When you need to type, press {key:i} to enter **insert mode**. Type what you need. Press {key:Esc} to come back. *Normal mode* is your resting state — the mode you spend most of your time in.

The productivity payoff isn't really about speed — it's about fluency. You stop translating between *what* you want to do and *how* to do it. The editing language becomes invisible, like touch typing.

blocks.title: Vim Is a State Machine
blocks.text:
Vim is a finite state machine. The states are the modes — *normal*, *insert*, *visual* (with three sub-states: char / line / block), *replace*, *command-line*, *operator-pending*, *terminal*. Every keystroke is interpreted *relative to the current state*. {key:i} in *normal mode* enters *insert mode*; in *insert mode* it inserts the letter `i`. The transition function is, in effect, the entire Vim manual.

This is why learning Vim feels different from learning an editor. You're learning a state machine. Once you have the states in your head, each key is just an edge in the graph.

blocks.title: Ken Thompson and the grandparent of modal editing
blocks.text:
Modal editing did not start with `vi`. It started with `ed`, the line editor Ken Thompson wrote for the very first Unix in 1969. `ed` had two modes: a *command* mode where single letters meant operations — {key:p} for print, {key:d} for delete, {key:s} for substitute — and an *input* mode you entered with {key:a} (append) or {key:i} (insert) and left by typing a single dot on its own line.

Ken worked on a Teletype Model 33 — no screen, just paper rolling out under a print head. Every byte of communication was expensive; every keystroke audible. A program that let one letter mean one verb wasn't a stylistic preference; it was the only design that fit the machine. `vi` — the direct ancestor of Vim — was the *visual* mode eventually built on top of `ex`, the descendant of `ed`. The colon prompt you reach in Vim by pressing {key::} is the direct great-grandchild of Ken's *command mode* — same single-letter verbs, same modal separation between *talking to the editor* and *adding text to the file*. Half a century later, you still type {key:d} and get a delete because Ken Thompson once had to save bytes and paper on a teletype.

blocks.caption: A Teletype Model 33 (ASR-33) — no screen, just paper. Ken Thompson wrote `ed`, the ancestor of `vi`, on a machine like this.
blocks.credit: Photo: Jud McCranie, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/), via Wikimedia Commons.

blocks.text: Every other editor starts in what Vim calls *insert mode*: keys type text. Vim starts in what every other editor might call *command mode*: keys do things. The learning curve is real. New users press {key:j} expecting a `j` and instead see the cursor move. They press {key:dd} expecting two letters and the line vanishes.

Push through this. Once you internalize the grammar — once {key:d} feels like *delete* and {key:j} feels like *down* — you stop thinking in keystrokes and start thinking in edits and motions: "delete this word," "change inside these quotes," "yank this paragraph," "jump to the matching bracket." Your hands just execute.

The {key:Esc} habit is the single most important reflex. After every thought, press {key:Esc}. Make it automatic. Your default state is Normal, not Insert. Each {key:Esc} also closes an undo unit, so {key:u} walks you back one thought at a time.

blocks.title: The Philosophical Bet Behind Modal Editing
blocks.text:
Modal editing is unusual enough that it is worth saying explicitly what bet you are making when you adopt it. The bet is: *the cost of having to switch modes once per editing session is smaller than the cost of having every keystroke be a typed character.*

In a non-modal editor, every key is a letter, so anything that is not a letter — moving, deleting, copying, searching — has to be a chord ({key:Ctrl}, {key:Shift}, {key:Alt}, sometimes all three). You pay for this on every command. Your hands chord constantly.

In a modal editor, you pay once: you press {key:i} to start typing, {key:Esc} to stop. In between, *every key on the keyboard is a command* — over 100 single-keystroke commands available without modifiers. The trade is: one mode switch per insertion in exchange for hundreds of unmodified single-keystroke commands during navigation and editing.

For people who edit text more than they type new text — which is most working programmers — this trade pays off enormously. The cost of the mode switch is small (one keystroke) and bounded; the savings from unmodified single-key commands compound across thousands of edits per day. Over a year, the difference is measured in hours.

blocks.section: Modal Editing — and the rest of the editing language
